data.title: Iovance Biotherapeutics: Positioned For Multi-Year Growth, Buy The Pullback
data.description:
Shares of Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA) have lost nearly two-thirds of their value since Amtagvi was approved in February 2024. On the other hand, shares were up by as much as 80% YTD mid-March due to increasing enthusiasm for Amtagvi's commercial prospects in melanoma along with accelerating clinical momentum plus improving margins (transitioning from science project to viable business).

Current pullback puts YTD gain at +20% and is likely due in part to aspiring competitor Replimune (REPL), with the April 10th PDUFA date coming up for RP1 in melanoma (combined with Bristol's PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor nivolumab).

While it's possible IOVA could experience a gap down if RP1 gets approved, I think bears and skeptics are missing the forest for the trees as the overall story continues to get better.

Chart

Figure 1: IOVA daily chart (Source: Finviz)

In the chart above, we can see shares were rangebound for much of the past year (stuck at around $2.50). In late February, they jumped on the Q4 update and ran to $5.50 before experiencing a gap down followed by a slow decline to $3.30.

My suggestion for readers who like this name is to accumulate weakness in the near term with an eye toward sales growth and pipeline progress during the 2026 to 2028 time period.

Select Recent Developments

Real-world results for Amtagvi were shared in an oral presentation at ASTCT and CIBMTR annual meetings. Physician-assessed ORR was 44% with a 73% disease control rate in 41 patients. Of note, responses were higher when patients had two or fewer prior lines of therapy (52% ORR). Both of these figures are significantly better than the 31% ORR reported from the pivotal trial and could boost uptake in less heavily pretreated melanoma patients as physician enthusiasm rises.

Figure 2: 5-year data from pivotal melanoma data showcases impressive durability that competitors will have to match (Source: corporate slides)

In the Q4 report released February 24th, the theme was increasing demand for Amtagvi driving sales growth, improved margins, and accelerating pipeline momentum. Q4 revenue was $87M ($65M Amtagvi, $22M Proleukin), representing 30% growth over the prior quarter. Q4 gross margin from cost of sales was 50% (increased cost optimization). Full-year 2025 total product revenue of $264M slightly exceeded guidance.

With $303M of cash on hand, management guided for operational runway into Q3 2027 (sounds like a secondary offering is likely in the next quarter or two to extend that). Encouragingly, they continue to guide for continued improvement in operating expenses, cost of sales, and gross margin throughout 2026 and into 2027.

Referral trends toward treating earlier-line melanoma patients are strengthening, with adoption increasing due to real-world response rates (captures the true value-add of Amtagvi better than the original pivotal trial results, which were obfuscated by treating later-line patients). ATC network is growing nicely, with further acceleration expected, and community ATCs driving demand this year and next. Specialty distribution channel was introduced as another option for ATCs to purchase Amtagvi.

As for geographic expansion, regulatory reviews are underway in the UK and Australia, with potential approvals in 1H 26 (Switzerland to follow in 1H 27). EMA resubmission is also expected this year.

Updated data for NSCLC is expected at a major medical meeting this year along with completion of enrollment for a pivotal trial (BLA submission to follow with potential launch in 2H 27). This is important, given the opportunity is much larger than the initial advanced melanoma market.

A new indication was uncovered with UPS and DDLPS (sarcoma subtypes) representing another path to market (encouraging 50% ORR in the first six evaluable patients). A single-arm registrational study in previously treated patients will get underway in Q2 2026, followed by engagement with FDA on a faster path to approval (I like how this is being prioritized). Devil's advocate, I don't like how we didn't hear anything on endometrial cancer data (makes me wonder if this is a "bait and switch" type scenario).

Enrollment is accelerating for the phase 3 frontline melanoma study for Amtagvi, and next-gen products (PD-1 inactivated and IL-2 analog) continue in earlier-stage studies. IL-12 tethered TIL is slated to enter phase 1/2 study 2H 26. Several investigator-sponsored studies are ongoing for new solid tumor indications (perhaps additional fast paths to market will be discovered, as was the case for sarcoma). This latter point is an underappreciated aspect of the Iovance story, in my opinion.

Focusing on the sarcoma opportunity, there are over 3,000 patients in the US and 5,000 in Europe with the UPS and DDLPS subtypes (high-grade & aggressive). Unmet need here is unfortunately very high, with less than 5% ORR for 2nd-line treatment, median PFS of 2-3 months, and median overall survival of 9 to 10 months (contrast to 50% ORR for Amtagvi). I see this as a blockbuster opportunity, but let's peg peak sales at $500M to be conservative. It should also be relatively feasible to expand out into additional sarcoma subtypes from there. It sounds like the FDA would be equally (or even more) amenable to single-arm studies supporting accelerated approval for both UPS and DDLPS as well as additional subtypes (I don't see why related indications wouldn't work based on initial data).

Here are a few nuggets from the conference call that stood out to me:

  • Management highlighted 30% revenue growth with the best-ever 50% margin from cost of sales.
  • They are projecting $1B+ peak sales for Amtagvi + Proleukin, and enrollment volumes are accelerating within their ATC network.
  • Operational improvements are evident throughout the entire Amtagvi treatment journey, from finding patients to manufacturing product to infusion.
  • Long-term vision is massive expansion of their TIL platform into new indications (supporting growth for years to come).

Figure 3: Market opportunity across solid tumors (Source: corporate slides)

  • They are in the early stages of penetrating the community market, which will unlock significant potential (could result in better referrals to melanoma doctors).
  • Real-world data is resonating with medical oncologists, leading to tissue procurement earlier on in the patient journey, including for specific subtypes (i.e., those with BRAF mutation before they suffer health declines).
  • Per management, Amtagvi has the potential to reach over 30,000 patients annually in the previously treated advanced melanoma market alone as global expansion continues (still sounds incredibly rosy to me).
  • Interestingly, the NSCLC opportunity is 7 times larger, with 50,000 addressable patients for peak sales of $10 billion in the US alone (at the current valuation, even achieving 5% of that number would be a significant achievement). What's cool here is this launch would be different, as the heavy lifting (building out the ATC network) has already been accomplished for melanoma. They could leverage the existing ATC network (won't have to build it out again).
  • Again, management confirmed that updated data for NSCLC will be presented at a major medical meeting this year
  • For sarcoma, based on prior approvals in various subtypes, the pivotal study size could be just 30 to 60 patients (more likely ~40 patients). I like how efficient this sounds in terms of capital spend and timeline.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, at a $1.1 billion enterprise value on the pullback, I think that shares of Iovance are currently being discounted due to such concerns as competition or perhaps the need to access funding in the near term.

The company clearly fits our selection criteria for the Core Biotech portfolio, with Amtagvi sales set to pick up in coming quarters and over a multi-year timeframe along with improving margins. Pipeline momentum is also encouraging, with pivotal lung cancer data this year and possible approval next year, then the newest indication of sarcoma to follow (potentially endometrial cancer after that).

Risks to the thesis should not be overlooked, such as dilutive secondary offering in the next quarter or two. Shares could also be pressured if competitor RP1 from Replimune receives approval soon, though I again note this therapy is being combined with PD-1, and some doctors are still skeptical about PD-1 retreatment (versus employing an entirely novel mechanism of action). Lung cancer data could worsen with higher number of patients or longer follow-up, as could sarcoma, which is starting from a very small base of patients from the initial readout. Competition from much more convenient or efficacious next-generation treatments is something to keep an eye on as well, such as Immatics (IMTX) with interim and final phase 3 data for cell therapy candidate Anzu-cel this year and BLA submission slated for 2027.

data.title: Wabtec: Full Steam Ahead
data.description:
Shares of Wabtec (WAB) have been trading strong, currently changing hands at just over $250 per share, within imminent reach of all-time highs. Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies benefits from continued organic growth, solid M&A efforts, and a strong order intake, firing on all cylinders here.

On top of strong current operating momentum, which has been going on for a couple of years, further growth is seen in sales and margins in the coming years. This provides a solid roadmap for the company to execute upon and even enjoy the ride. However, with shares trading near the all-time highs, for good reasons, quite some good news has been priced in here, with shares trading around 25 times forward earnings.

This makes me appreciative of the performance of the business, yet I am hard-pressed to see appeal here, although I am seriously considering serious setbacks here. My regard for Wabtec has only been increasing. After I was constructive on the shares in the fall at levels just below the $200 mark, shares have risen by a quarter ever since, killing the immediate appeal.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Good News Show

In February, Wabtec announced the closure of the $960 million acquisition of Dellner-Couplers, a Swedish-based firm whose acquisition was announced early last year.

Other good news was reported on the order front. In February, the company reported a $670 million deal with CSX (CSX) to upgrade a fleet of 100 new Evolution Series locomotives and 50 modernized locomotives. That news came days after the company signed a $1.2 billion deal with Union Pacific (UNP) to modernize its AC4400 locomotives. In fact, a third order was reported this quarter, with Wabtec announcing a smaller $386 million deal with the New York MTA back in January. Combined, these orders total $2.2 billion—that's a substantial number, almost equivalent to a quarter's worth of sales.

Meanwhile, Wabtec reported solid results for the year 2025 in the middle of February. Full-year sales rose by nearly 8% to $11.2 billion, with fourth-quarter sales seen up nearly 15% to levels just shy of $3.0 billion, with revenues trending near $12 billion each year.

Full-year GAAP operating margins rose by more than a point to 18% of sales. This is even after a $300 million amortization charge, with adjusted margins seen in the low twenties. Adjusted earnings rose by 19% to $8.97 per share, with this adjustment looking fair in relation to a $6.83 per share number based on GAAP accounting, really driven by amortization charges.

Net debt was reported at $4.75 billion, that all being very reasonable with EBITDA seen near $2.5 billion for the year, as leverage is seen just below 2 times, that ahead of the Dellner-Couplers deal closing in the first quarter.

The order intake and momentum seen in the first quarter of 2026 were already seen in the 2025 numbers. While the 12-month backlog rose by a solid 7% to $8.2 billion, it really was a 23% increase in the total backlog to $27.4 billion, which was impressive.

Full Steam Ahead

After this solid year, Wabtec showed no signs of slowing down, with 2026 sales seen moving up to $12.19-$12.49 billion, up around 10%. Moreover, continued operating leverage is seen with adjusted earnings up to $10.05-$10.45 per share, up 12-16% per year.

Further, returns to investors are also seen growing with the board upping the buyback program to $1.2 billion, as dividends have been hiked by 24% to $0.31 per share, for an annual payout of $1.24 per share. While the improvements are spectacular, current payouts provide just a 0.5% dividend yield here.

The strong order intake in the first quarter provides real support in the 2026 guidance, all of which is quite encouraging and thus has been driving shares higher. This comes as Wabtec has become a giant, and after struggling a bit in the years following the GE Transportation merger, real operating momentum has been seen more recently, driven by M&A but also outright strong execution.

What Now?

Back in the fall, I believed that earnings power would only approach $10 per share in 2026, but now it becomes obvious that the company will likely top this with ease. This has boosted the shares, which have risen from levels around the $200 mark in November to $253 right now, strongly outperforming the wider market.

The share price gains outweigh the near-term boost to earnings per share, and based on the guidance for this year, shares have re-rated to 24-25 times forward earnings. That is a rather steep multiple for a company that has been trading around 20 times earnings in more recent times.

Moreover, the outlook remains good, as the societies across the globe are battling with bad traffic, providing structural advantages of rail transportation, as well as demand for transit systems as well, with that segment responsible for 30% of Wabtec's sales. While transit carries lower margins and slower growth, congestion and expensive fuel might provide medium-term drivers here.

On the back of the good news, a lot has been priced into the stock here. Trading around the $90 mark when the company announced its merger with GE Transportation in 2018, the company and thus shares really had to digest that deal, this still being a $100 stock until 2023.

Massive momentum made shares double to $200 by year-end 2024. Continued momentum was seen over the past year. This is driven by a combination of organic growth, solid capital returns in between buybacks, dividends, M&A, margin gains, and strong order intake. This makes investors feel comfortable awarding higher multiples to the business, with the runway for growth seen as longer and stronger.

That is clearly what management is guiding for, making investors upbeat here with the track record in place in recent years. For the coming five years, the company sees mid-single-digit organic sales growth, with about 350 basis points of operating margin gains seen. This creates a roadmap for earnings to advance by ten percentage points or so for years.

That is very encouraging, certainly as the North American railcar-built market is seen as soft (again) in 2026, after 2025 was a softer year already. This only accelerates the aging of active locomotives, with a quarter of locomotives in operation for 20 years or older. This, of course, drives near-term demand for maintenance, equipment, and eventually modernization efforts here.

Despite the strong run in the shares, which is fully supported by the strong performance of the business and outlook, I am leaning cautious. This strong performance has come at a time when the market at large has seen some setbacks, marking great outperformance. This makes me very appreciative of the business and the stock, and while I expect continued strength and support of the shares, I fail to see a compelling risk-reward here.

data.title: Assertio: Why I'm Selling The Rally (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Assertio Overview

My last look at Assertio (ASRT) was in September 2023. A key company product, Indocin, faced new generic competition. But Assertio was no stranger to generic entries and had a few products to offset some losses from Indocin. Back then, Assertio was growing net product sales by 13% and was cash generative.

When I wrote this piece, the stock was amid a large drop. After reaching >$100/share just months earlier, ASRT traded in the $40s (Note: there was a 1-for-15 reverse stock split in December). I rated ASRT a "Hold," but cautioned that "potential further decline" was possible "despite sparkling valuation metrics."

As is often the case in the stock market, "cheap" stocks are "cheap" for good reason and have a tendency to stay "cheap." Indeed, ASRT has fallen another 61% versus S&P 500 (SPY) gains of 46%.

Zooming in, ASRT has, however, rallied 90% in the past year.

Below, I take another look to see if there remains "wind behind Assertio's back."

Recent Performance

I always pay close attention to Seeking Alpha's Quant ratings, and ASRT ranks 32 overall out of 4266 tickers. Again, ASRT sports a sparkling "A-" grade on Valuation. Its P/E Non-GAAP (forward) is just 5.11. The sector (Health Care) median is 17.14.

ASRT also earns good scores in Growth (B; 81% YoY EBITDA Growth), Profitability (B-; Gross Profit Margin 70.19%), and Momentum (aka "stock performance").

What does this all mean?

I'd argue that a P/E of 5.11 signals that the market expects earnings to collapse further. Assertio has not been able to offset the losses of its most profitable product, Indocin.

Recall that Rolvedon was an acquired asset. It is quickly becoming "all Assertio has." Rolvedon now accounts for ~58% of total revenue. Any pricing pressure or market share loss for Rolvedon could leave Assertio with very little.

Moreover, Assertio's FY25 performance requires some nuance.

It was artificially buoyed by a "sell-in" event in Q3. The company pushed five quarters' worth of Rolvedon wholesaler shipments to manage a distribution transition. This, in large part, accounted for the "81% YoY EBITDA Growth."

Regarding the 70.19% Gross Profit Margin, this is actually relatively modest for a specialty pharma company compared to peers like Catalyst (CPRX), Harmony (HRMY), and Corcept (CORT).

Assertio's margins are also trending lower, with hopes that they have bottomed.

Then, of course, Momentum grades can simply reflect a "dead cat bounce" or temporary volatility rather than a fundamental turnaround.

Speaking of which, are there any actual signs of a turnaround?

Management's guidance did provide some early signs.

For the first time since the Indocin generic entry, management provided a clear floor for profitability. They are also busy "right-sizing" cost structures, as evidenced by diminishing SG&A expenses. Finally, the "noise" from the Spectrum acquisition is largely over. As I often point out in my analyses, it can take years to fully integrate an acquisition.

On the flip side, Assertio's cash is dwindling, GAAP net losses are accumulating ($30.4M in 2025), and further Indocin erosion is expected in 2026.

Assertio's "Altman Z Score," which, according to Seeking Alpha, "may be used to predict the probability that a firm will go into bankruptcy within two years," is -2.81, falling materially short of the "above 3" of companies that "are not likely to go bankrupt."

Assertio's below-zero score is a feature of accumulated deficit (from years of restructuring and acquisitions), operating losses, and a market capitalization (~$120M) that is less than Assertio's total liabilities ($173M).

Management Commentary

During the call, management revealed that FY26 will only reflect three quarters of Rolvedon shipments (beginning Q2). This will give the appearance of a massive YoY acceleration compared to the artificial "zero" they created in Q4 '25 and Q1 '26.

When it comes to strategy, management confirmed that on-market products are "no longer capital efficient" and that Assertio is shifting towards "development stage" assets.

In other words, the company has run out of money to buy meaningful, revenue-generating products like Rolvedon. This is a telltale sign that Assertio's operations are shifting from "specialty pharma" to "biotech speculation," albeit later-stage assets. And, of course, the latter is also associated with increasing costs (i.e., R&D).

Rolvedon

Because Assertio is essentially a "Rolvedon company with a few diminishing assets," I wanted to take the time to review how robust this franchise is.

Rolvedon, as a franchise, has some good features and some bad.

As management loves to point out, its IP extends to 2039. It has its own unique permanent J-code (J1449). And it is established in NCCN and ASCO guidelines.

The problem is that Rolvedon is just a "challenger" brand in a competitive market.

Amgen's (AMGN) Neulasta Onpro is a passive on-body injector that allows patients to go home and have the drug automatically injected 24 hours later. Whereas Rolvedon is currently a prefilled syringe that requires patients to return to the clinic the next day.

Assertio's partner, Hanmi, is counteracting with an auto-injector (still requires active patient self-administration) that is expected to launch in the 1H '26 in South Korea (Assertio has U.S. rights). But it still doesn't match the "set it and forget it" convenience of Amgen's Onpro.

Then you also have to bear in mind that there are now over half a dozen biosimilars competing on price.

Rolvedon's US market is also purposefully niche. Management opted to focus primarily on community oncology clinics.

This limits TAM. So I wouldn't expect Rolvedon to suddenly become a major growth catalyst for Assertio, even with a more convenient auto-injector eventually coming to the US (management appears to be maximizing the prefilled syringe in community clinics for now).

Financial Health

At the end of '25, Assertio had $63.4M in cash and investments.

In Q4, "cash burn" (cash from operations) was negative $30M. However, this was impacted by one-offs (i.e., working capital expansion). To ascertain "cash runway," which, to my knowledge, the company did not estimate itself, it would be more prudent to look at cash from operations in the trailing twelve months (negative $28.2M). If we divide Assertio's cash and investments by this figure, it implies a cash runway of roughly nine quarters.

The company may need to raise more funds within a couple of years, which could involve substantial dilution at current prices.

Risks

Although I've covered many Assertio-specific risks already within the body of my analysis, I'd also like to take a moment to address some microcap-specific risks.

Due to a market capitalization of just ~$120M, ASRT investors should also be aware of risks such as low liquidity, dilution, volatility, a total loss of investment, and limited public information.

Challenges to Thesis

Some things that could challenge my bearish thesis include:

  • Assertio reaching GAAP profitability
  • Rolvedon auto-injector success
  • Margin stabilization
  • Accretive M&A
  • Indocin revenue floor
  • Cash reserve growth
  • Organic EBITDA growth

Conclusion

My cautious "Hold" recommendation back in 2023 supposed that Assertio would be able to somewhat offset/slow down Indocin's LOE, but the exact opposite is happening. While Rolvedon has buoyed things to some extent, it does not appear positioned for meaningful growth in the future. And Assertio's $63.4M in cash and investments is no longer enough to immediately offset Indocin-related losses via M&A. Instead, the company has its eyes on assets still in development, which entails high clinical risk and R&D expenditures. Lastly, Assertio's sparkling Quant grades appear to be a mirage.

So, I'd use this opportunity to sell (downgrade ASRT to "Sell").

data.title: Employment Report: 178K Jobs Added In March, Better Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 178,000 jobs were added in March, up from February's 133,000 loss. This figure was better than the projected addition of 65,000 jobs and marks the largest gain since December 2024. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate inched down to 4.3%, coming in below the forecasted 4.4% rate.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 178,000 in March, and the unemployment rate changed little at 4.3 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Job gains occurred in health care, in construction, and in transportation and warehousing. Federal government employment continued to decline.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.3 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.2 million, changed little in March. These measures also changed little over the year.

Establishment Survey Data:Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 178,000 in March, following a decrease in February (-133,000). In March, job gains occurred in health care, in construction, and in transportation and warehousing. Federal government employment continued to decline. Payroll employment had changed little on net over the prior 12 months.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last five years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 68,000, the highest level since April 2025.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 22,000.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over. The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.256% (to three decimal places), the lowest level since June 2025.

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.26%, lower than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.31%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong.

The most recent trough occurred 33 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but quickly fell. This rate has recently been trending upwards and now stands at 1.1%.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 25.3 weeks, down from 25.7 in February. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work. Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions.

In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks. The U6 series currently stands at 8.0%, up from 7.9% in February.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 61.9%, its lowest level since the end of 2021.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 6.9%, down from 7.2% in February.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. The latest reading is at 59.2%, the lowest level since the end of 2021.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
